title: AuditLogger swallows every error — auditing is best-effort with no alerting
description:
Steps to reproduce:
1. 

Expected: 
Actual: 

Dimension: Error Orchestration (7) / Security and Access (5)

Location: src/session/audit.rs (entire module).

Violation: An audit log is a security-critical primitive. This implementation treats every failure as recoverable and hides it from the caller:

1. new() prints to stderr with eprintln!() and returns a no-op logger (file: None) on mkdir failure OR on open failure (lines 17, 28). The caller has no way to know the audit log is disabled — log() silently does nothing.
2. log() wraps the write in 'writeln!(f, ...).ok()' at line 45, discarding any I/O error. Disk full? Audit log on wrong filesystem? You will NEVER KNOW.
3. No fsync. The audit log sits in the OS page cache until the process exits or Linux flushes; a crash loses entries.
4. File is opened append-only at construction and held for the session. If log rotation external to this program renames the file (logrotate), writes continue to the now-deleted inode until the FD closes — standard UNIX gotcha; logs are silently lost.
5. No rate limiting. A hostile tool loop could explode the audit file without bound (relates to the turn_metrics unbounded issue #285).
6. eprintln!() is used for the failure path instead of tracing::warn!() (which is used elsewhere in the codebase, e.g. session/mod.rs:370). Inconsistent error-handling style.

Mandated Refactor:
1. new() returns Result<Self, AuditError>. Caller decides whether an un-auditable run is acceptable.
2. log() returns Result<(), io::Error>. Caller must handle (or explicitly drop).
3. Use tracing::warn!() not eprintln!(), per the rest of the codebase.
4. fsync after each log entry OR at a configurable batch interval (default every 10 entries or 1 second).
5. Handle SIGHUP to re-open the file for logrotate compatibility.
6. Add a fallback: if the primary file fails, buffer last 100 entries in memory and attempt re-open on the next log() call.
